title: The Pila-Zannier Strategy for Drinfeld Modules
speaker: Francesco Maria Saettone
abstract: The Pila–Zannier strategy has proved to be a particularly fruitful approach: first applied to reprove the Manin–Mumford conjecture, it was later exploited decisively in proofs of the André–Oort conjecture. Crucially relying on a recent counting theorem of Binyamini–Kato specialized to function fields of positive characteristic, we extend this method to the setting of Drinfeld modules, thereby (re)proving certain cases of these conjectures. Joint work in progress with Gal Binyamini and Dmitry Novikov.

proof_stages.label: Function-field Drinfeld-module framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes A=F_q[t] and the completed algebraically closed field C_infinity, defines Drinfeld modules, their products, sub-Drinfeld modules, analytic uniformization by lattices and exponentials, and the torsion sets on which unlikely-intersection statements will be formulated.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic lower bounds for special points
proof_stages.summary: The Pila-Zannier lower-bound side is supplied first by Breuer/Masser-type Galois orbit growth for Drinfeld torsion points, and later by class-number/Siegel lower bounds for CM Drinfeld modules.

proof_stages.label: Rigid-analytic Pila-Wilkie upper bounds
proof_stages.summary: A rigid-analytic Pila-Wilkie theorem, together with a block refinement for fibers, gives sub-polynomial upper bounds for rational points of bounded height outside the algebraic part.

proof_stages.label: Ax-Lindemann-Weierstrass for Drinfeld exponentials
proof_stages.summary: For a maximal irreducible component in the preimage of an algebraic subvariety under the Drinfeld exponential, functional transcendence says its image is weakly special; in the explained rank-one model this is proved by monodromy plus lattice-counting arguments that force the defining subgroup relation to be linear.

proof_stages.label: Manin-Mumford via the Pila-Zannier contradiction
proof_stages.summary: For an irreducible subvariety of a product of two same-rank Drinfeld modules with Zariski-dense torsion, torsion points are lifted to an affinoid parameter space, block counting is compared with Galois lower bounds, and nonconstant blocks are ruled out by Ax-Lindemann unless the variety is a torsion translate of a sub-Drinfeld module.

proof_stages.label: Andre-Oort adaptation for rank-two Drinfeld modular curves
proof_stages.summary: The same strategy is sketched for curves in the Drinfeld j-line squared: CM orbit lower bounds, a hyperbolic Ax-Lindemann theorem, and Taguchi-height control of the cusp combine with rigid counting to force a curve with infinitely many CM points to be special.

examples.name: Elliptic-curve and multiplicative-group analogies
examples.note: Elliptic curves, abelian varieties, and G_m are used as familiar characteristic-zero comparators for Drinfeld-module products, uniformization, and the contrast between complicated group law and complicated module action.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses elliptic curves and the multiplicative group only as analogies for Drinfeld modules and contrasts their group/module structures without doing computations on them.

examples.name: Graph of Frobenius
examples.note: The curve given by the Frobenius graph illustrates that one projection to the lattice is too sparse, revealing the counting obstruction that forces matching degrees/ranks.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes the graph of Frobenius by intersecting it with the lattice, projecting to the two factors, and using the sparsity of p-th powers to get a contradiction.

passes.reason: The Frobenius graph is used as a bad/additive case whose lattice intersection is too sparse, so it is ruled out by counting.

examples.name: Three rank-two factors versus rank-three Drinfeld moduli
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker contrasts products of copies of the rank-two Drinfeld modular line with the genuinely different moduli space of rank-three Drinfeld modules.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker only notes that three rank-two factors differ from rank-three Drinfeld moduli despite both looking like affine 3-space, without applying any construction or calculation.
